Player is attempting to reframe the moral/practical argument and extract information that could shift Orrath's stance. Outcome is uncertain; success/failure meaningfully affects group dynamics and next moves.

turns.playerInput:
I said to him: It means we also need the shrine components to start the testimony so the Willspring can exist in peace within the mountains. No one would listen to us unless we show them we have the ability to make changes. We take one of it, let her come to us and force her to be on the table with us.

Then I ask him to wait aside, take the spear and walk straight into the camp, guards are not a problem. The first guard notice me and draw his sword out and yelling at me to stop. I keep approaching, blocked his sword, Stab his wrist and disarmed him. The second guard was knocked out by spear before he aware what is going on. I entered the tent take one component. I go out to tell the first guard who's covering his hand with blood that I will go to talk to his master. Tell her to wait for me.
turns.narration:
You explain it to Orrath in a single clean sentence — the shrine-component is not theft, it is a demonstration. Without the ability to change something inside Vorn's certainty, the three-source convergence remains a scholar's argument. You will take one component and let the absence speak first. Then she will come to the table because she has no other choice.

Orrath receives this. He does not agree and he does not argue. He steps off the path and sits on a rock with the stillness of a man who has already said what he had to say and will now witness what comes next. That is the only answer he gives.

You take the spear and walk straight into camp.

The first guard clocks you at thirty yards — a stranger, no Conclave blue, carrying a spear on the approach path at last light. He draws and shouts the order to stop. You do not stop. The distance closes at your pace, not his, and when he swings the blade up to bar your way your left hand is already there — the spear's haft catches his wrist on the rising arc, not a strike, a redirection, and his sword pivots out of the grip as his wrist bends further than it wants to. He gasps. The blade hits the granite. You step past him. The second guard is moving from the tent-line to the right, hand on his weapon — he has not fully processed what he just saw — and the spear's shaft catches him across the temple with the flat economy of a form that does not waste motion. He goes down without completing the draw. Clean. No one is dead.

The warding tent is identifiable by the pale threadwork on the canvas and the faint luminous pressure at the entrance seam — the same amber resonance as the Fault-veins but sealed, contained, human-made. Inside: four stone components on cloth-covered stands, each attended by its own small geometric seal drawn in ley-chalk. The warmth of them is not the Wellspring's warmth. Closer to an echo. You take the smallest — a grey-green cardinal stone, perhaps palm-sized — and feel the seal break as it leaves the stand. The remaining three pulse once, brighter, and hold. The alarm is already moving.

You walk back out into the cooling mountain air. The first guard is on his knees, his sword arm pressed to his chest, blood welling between his fingers. You crouch briefly to his eye level and speak in plain trade-tongue: go to your Archmagister and tell her a man came down from the Auric ravine, took the eastern component, disarmed her wardens without killing them, and is waiting at Helvrath's Cross waystation to speak with her. Tell her he knows what is wrong with the ritual.

You rise, pocket the component — it sits against your ribs, close to the Shard, and the two frequencies do not harmonize — and walk back toward the path where Orrath is waiting.
turns.ask: Orrath is on the rock where you left him. He has heard the sounds from the camp. The component is in your possession. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Say nothing to Orrath — keep walking toward the King's Road and let him fall in if he chooses
Tell Orrath plainly: the door is open now; we move to Helvrath's Cross before the wardens regroup
Hand the component to Orrath to carry — it should not sit against the Shard all the way down the mountain
Ask Orrath how long before Conclave reinforcements could reach Helvrath's Cross ahead of Vorn herself
Pause and feel whether the Shard has any reaction to the component's proximity — what the two frequencies do to each other

diceResult.reason: Combat against armed guards with intent to disarm and neutralize without killing. Multiple simultaneous threats (first guard's sword, second guard's awareness) require precise, risky execution. High stakes: failure means alarm raised, reinforcements, mission compromised.

turns.playerInput: Tell Orrath plainly: the door is open now; we move to Helvrath's Cross before the wardens regroup
turns.narration:
You return to where Orrath sits on his rock at the path's edge. He has not moved. His eyes are on the staging camp's lantern-blue, reading the shape of the alarm — the doubled warden-movement at the tent-line, the single figure running toward the warding tent. He already knows what happened inside. He has been sitting with it.

You tell him plainly: the door is open now. We move to Helvrath's Cross before the wardens regroup.

Orrath is quiet for a moment. Not the naming-silence — something older and harder than that. Then he rises from the rock in a single unhurried motion and adjusts his pack across his shoulders. He looks at you directly, the rare full look, and holds it for one breath. 'The door is open,' he says. 'Yes.' He does not say: and I told you the cost. He does not say: I do not know whether this was right. He says the true thing only: 'She will come angry. And afraid. The combination is not a door — it is a blade in someone else's hand.' He pauses. 'We should be at Helvrath's Cross before she chooses what to do with it.' That is all. He turns to the path.

You move. The High Approach gives way to the descent road quickly — steeper here than the ravine path, the granite underfoot loose-sheeted and requiring attention. The last of the day's light is going amber-grey at the mountain's western shoulder. Below, the King's Road is a pale line threading south toward Helvrath's Cross, perhaps two hours at a moving pace. The eastern cardinal stone sits against your ribs beside the Shard. The two frequencies do not harmonize — a faint dissonance, low and persistent, like a string tuned a quarter-step wrong. You carry it without adjusting to it. Behind you, the staging camp's lantern-blue is brightening as the dark comes on. Someone is already moving on the upper path with a torch.

turns.playerInput: Ask Orrath what he knows of Helvrath's Cross — its layout, who shelters there, what ground it gives you
turns.narration:
The descent road levels briefly where the granite gives way to packed earth — the mountain releasing you to the foothills' grammar. The last amber light is gone from the peaks; the sky above the western shoulder holds the deep blue of a day that is finished but not yet night. The torch behind you on the upper path has not gained ground. You have the interval.

You ask Orrath what he knows of Helvrath's Cross as you walk — its layout, who shelters there, what ground it gives you.

Orrath does not slow his pace. He speaks into the path ahead, the trade-tongue precise and without decoration. 'A waystation. Old — older than the Conclave's claim on the mountain road. Built where two trade-routes cross: the King's Road coming west from Veranthos, and the southern spur that drops toward the Thornwood lowlands.' A pause, footfall on compressed earth. 'One inn. Stone-built, two stories. The sign is a bull's head — the family name is Ceth, and the daughter runs it now, the father died two winters past. She does not ask about cargo.' He says this last part the way he says everything that is technical information — without emphasis, because the emphasis is already in the content. 'A stable. A well. An old Covenant waymarker at the crossing itself — the kind the First Archmages placed along the ley-road before the binding settled. The stone still hums on clear nights.' Another pause, slightly longer. 'Perhaps thirty people shelter there at any given time. Traders off the King's Road, a few who move between the Thornwood lowland bands and Veranthos. Some who are neither and say little.' He glances at you once, sideways, the brief direct look. 'The Grey-Faction has used it. Not recently, to my knowledge. But the bisected-eye mark has been seen on cargo moving through.'

He is quiet for ten paces. Then: 'It gives you ground because it is not Conclave territory. The wardens do not maintain a post there — the innkeeper would not permit it and the Throne has not pressed the question. Vorn will come, if she comes, as a person without wardens behind her or she will come with wardens and announce to everyone sheltering there what she is protecting.' He stops — not the body, the speech. That is the whole account. 'Either tells you something about what she decides we are worth.' The path curves south. Somewhere below, invisible in the dark, Helvrath's Cross is waiting with its stone inn and its humming waymarker and its population of people who move between named things without belonging to any of them.

turns.playerInput: Enter the inn quietly, take a back table with sight-lines to both doors, and wait for Vorn without announcing yourself
turns.narration:
The King's Road finds you an hour south of the mountain's skirt — hard-packed earth, ley-lantern posts spaced wide enough that the dark pools between them. Helvrath's Cross announces itself before it is visible: woodsmoke, a mule's complaint, the low warm register of people who have stopped moving for the night. Then the waystation itself, a stone-built square at the crossing of two roads, the bull's-head sign swinging without wind above a door that lets out a blade of hearth-light when a trader shoulders through it.

You enter quietly. Not performing quiet — the body's default, the spear-master's habit of reading a room from the threshold before committing weight. The common room is low-ceilinged, timber-beamed, perhaps twenty people distributed across it in the configurations of people who do not know each other and are not trying to: three traders at the long table near the fire, two Thornwood-marked men playing something with tiles near the far wall, a woman in undyed wool asleep in her chair, a pair of Throne-liveried road-wardens eating without talking. The road-wardens clock you. They do not move. You are not wearing Conclave blue, you have not arrived at speed, and your spear is carried correctly. You are a traveler. They return to their food.

The back corner gives you what you need: a table that no one has chosen because it is away from the fire's warmth, which means it is also away from the fire's attention. From here the entry door is in full view. The side door — through the kitchen, you read it from the draft — is in partial view. Orrath takes the bench across from you without instruction, his back to the wall, his eyes on the room with the unhurried quality of a man who has been reading rooms since before you were born. The eastern cardinal stone sits against your ribs beside the Shard. The dissonance between them is low and persistent. You set the spear against the wall where it is reachable without being declarative.

The innkeeper — a woman in her mid-thirties, broad-shouldered, moving with the economy of someone who has been on her feet since before dawn — brings two cups of something hot without being asked. She looks at you for a half-second longer than she looks at other customers. Not alarm. Assessment. Then she moves back toward the bar. She has read that you are waiting for something, and has decided that is not her business tonight. Ceth's daughter. Her father dead two winters past. The inn still runs.

The waymarker is visible through the common room's single window — the old Covenant stone at the crossing outside, pale in the dark, humming at a frequency you feel rather than hear. The Shard registers it. You do not reach toward it. You settle your breath at the sternum, let the room resolve around you, and wait. Vorn may come tonight. She may come tomorrow. She may come with wardens or without them, and that answer will tell you more than the first words she speaks.
turns.ask: The room is yours for now — Orrath across from you, sight-lines to both doors, the stone humming outside. How do you spend the waiting hours before Vorn arrives?

Order food and eat — the body has climbed a mountain and fought two men; it requires fuel before another kind of fight
Speak quietly with Orrath about how to open the conversation with Vorn — what the first words should be, who speaks them

turns.playerInput: Order food and eat — the body has climbed a mountain and fought two men; it requires fuel before another kind of fight
turns.narration:
You catch Ceth's daughter on her next pass and order without ceremony — whatever is hot, whatever is ready, two portions. She reads the request for what it is: a working body's need, not a preference. She does not ask. She goes.

The food arrives in twelve minutes. A clay pot of something between stew and porridge — lentils, a root vegetable whose name you do not know in trade-tongue, a dark preserved meat that is not quite lamb. Two rounds of flatbread, slightly charred on one side. A refill of the hot cups without being asked. It is not a good meal. It is the correct meal. You eat with the same economy you apply to everything the body requires — steadily, without waste, clearing the bowl in the way of a man who has learned not to leave food when the next meal's timing is uncertain.

Orrath eats more slowly, in the shaman's manner, with the quality of attention he brings to things worth attending. He tears the flatbread precisely and does not speak. Across the room the two road-wardens have finished and are not leaving — they have ordered something to drink and settled their posture into the particular arrangement of men who have decided to stay without deciding why. One of them glances toward your corner twice in the span of five minutes. Not alarm. The trained habit of a warden reading a room for the thing that does not belong, finding you, and not yet naming what category you occupy.

The eastern cardinal stone against your ribs is a low persistent pressure — not painful, not warm the way the Shard is warm. A wrong frequency, patient and indifferent. The Shard beside it has not moved since the column stilled. The waymarker outside hums at a frequency you feel through the bench and the soles of your boots. Somewhere on the King's Road, a Conclave Archmagister has received a message from a bleeding warden. The mountain is above. The meal is in front of you. The body has what it needs. Now the waiting.
turns.ask: The meal is finished. The room holds — the road-wardens are lingering, the Thornwood men are still at their tiles, Orrath sits across from you in the back corner. Vorn has not arrived. What do you do?

Take the eastern cardinal stone from your pocket and set it on the table between you and Orrath — let it sit in the open where Vorn will see it when she enters
Step outside to the waymarker stone and let the Shard register the old Covenant frequency — read whether the two harmonize or conflict
Rest your eyes and let the body recover fully — conserve everything for the conversation that is coming

turns.playerInput: Ask Orrath quietly how he wants to position himself when Vorn arrives — whether he speaks or holds silence
turns.narration:
The common room has thinned since you ate. The three traders at the long table have gone up to rooms; one of the Thornwood tile-players is asleep in his chair, the other gone. The two road-wardens remain — a second round in front of them now, a deliberate patience that you read as professional rather than suspicious. They are waiting for something too, or the habit of waiting has become indistinguishable from the thing itself.

You ask Orrath quietly, across the table, how he wants to position himself when Vorn arrives. Whether he speaks or holds silence.

Orrath turns his cup in both hands once — the thinking gesture, the shaman's equivalent of a breath. He looks at the entry door, then at the side door through the kitchen, then back at the cup. When he speaks it is in the register he uses for things he has been deciding since before you asked.

'Silence.' He says it without hesitation, then qualifies it with precision. 'Not because I have nothing to say. Because I am Thornwood. The moment she sees me she will place us — the three delegations, the foothills boundary, eleven years of refusal. I become the argument she has already answered.' A pause, his thumbs still against the cup. 'If you carry the three-source convergence first, you are a stranger with a shrine-component she cannot explain. That is a question she has not answered. I am an answer she has already given.' He looks at you directly. 'When she can no longer close the question with the answer she prepared — then I speak. Not before.'

He sets the cup down. One more thing, quieter, without looking up: 'She will bring at least one warden. Probably two. They will read me as the reason you came down from the mountain with her property.' His hands open slightly on the table — the same small motion as the ravine floor, the burden-distributed motion. 'I will sit where I am sitting. I will not move unless I need to. This is also a kind of testimony.'

The waymarker outside hums through the bench and the soles of your boots. The eastern cardinal stone against your ribs sits in its wrong frequency, patient. The inn is quiet in the way of things that are waiting for their next shape.
turns.ask: Vorn has not yet arrived. The night is deep, the room is thinning, and Orrath has told you his position. What do you do while you wait?

Study the eastern cardinal stone quietly under the table — learn its frequency before Vorn demands it back
Watch the two road-wardens and read whether their patience is coincidence or whether Conclave word has already reached the King's Road

Prepare the three-source convergence argument in your mind — sequence the geography so it arrives in the right order when Vorn sits down

turns.playerInput: Study the eastern cardinal stone quietly under the table — learn its frequency before Vorn demands it back
turns.narration:
You draw the eastern cardinal stone into your lap beneath the table, out of the sightline of the two road-wardens, and hold it in both hands the way you hold the spear before a form — not gripping, receiving. The common room continues around you: the fire settling, the wardens murmuring something low, Ceth's daughter moving bottles behind the bar. Orrath reads what you are doing without looking directly at it and adjusts his posture — a fraction wider, a fraction more present — to block the wardens' angle. He does not look at you. He does not need to.

The stone is grey-green and palm-sized and cool in the way of something that has been indoors for a long time. Its surface is smooth except for the Conclave's ley-chalk geometry, now broken where the seal lifted — you can feel the interruption under your thumb, a line that no longer closes. Its frequency is not the Wellspring's frequency. That much you already knew from the walk down. What you learn now, holding it quietly in the dark, is more specific: it is an anchor. Not a key, not a living thing — an anchor for a shape. The shape it is anchored to is old. It predates the Conclave, predates Thessaly Vorn, predates the failing binding — it is a shape the First Archmages chose because it was singular and holdable and could be fixed in ley-chalk and stone. The shape of the Wellspring as it was a thousand years ago. The shape that no longer exists.

The Shard responds — not with warmth, not with gladness, but with something you can only name as recognition. The way a traveler recognizes a portrait of someone they knew, painted before that person became what they now are. The anchor and the Shard are not opposed. They are simply from different times. The anchor holds an answer to a question the Wellspring has already moved past.

You sit with this for a long time. The fire across the room pops once. Orrath lifts his cup. The stone in your lap is cool and precise and built for a world that is still technically the world — but only just. When you return it to your coat pocket and bring your hands back to the table, you know what you are holding: not a weapon, not a bargaining chip. A relic. An answer from before the question changed. Vorn has built her entire geometry around four of these, and she does not yet know they are portraits of a shape that has been dreaming itself into something else for a thousand years.
turns.ask: The common room is quiet. The wardens have not moved. The fire is low. Helvrath's Cross holds its waiting posture. What do you do?
suggestedActions: Sit with the knowledge — let the stillness hold until Vorn arrives; do not spend what you now know before the moment requires it
Speak quietly to Orrath: tell him what the stone's frequency revealed — that it anchors an old shape, a portrait of what the Wellspring was, not what it is
Watch the two road-wardens more carefully — read whether they are waiting for Vorn's arrival or simply lingering by professional habit
Step outside briefly to stand at the Covenant waymarker in the dark — let the stone's frequency and the marker's hum speak to each other, see what the interval tells you
Order something from Ceth's daughter and use the exchange to read her — whether she has already been approached by Conclave riders ahead of Vorn

turns.playerInput: Sit with the knowledge — let the stillness hold until Vorn arrives; do not spend what you now know before the moment requires it
turns.narration:
The fire across the room has burned down to a steady, considered heat — no longer throwing light, only holding it. The common room has thinned to its residue: the two road-wardens with their third round, a trader asleep against the wall with his pack beneath his feet, Ceth's daughter moving with diminished frequency behind the bar, wiping the same section of counter in the way of people who need something to do with their hands while they wait for the night to close.

You do not reach for the eastern cardinal stone again. You do not rehearse the three-source convergence or model Vorn's opening position or calculate the wardens' riding time from the staging camp. You let the knowledge sit where you put it — behind the sternum, alongside the Shard, in the body that climbed the mountain and answered the Wellspring's question and walked into a warded tent and came out the other side. The body knows it. The body will not forget it. Spending it now, in the dark, in your own mind, would only wear the edge.

Orrath has not moved in a long time. His cup is empty and he has not asked for another. His eyes are on the middle distance — not the door, not you, not the wardens — the particular quality of stillness that is not absence but a very full presence turned inward. The shaman's watch. You recognize it the way you recognize a form held correctly in someone else's body.

The Shard is quiet at your sternum. Not oriented, not reaching — arrived, resting, the warmth of it the same low even warmth it has held since the voreth-column stilled. The eastern cardinal stone in your coat pocket carries its discordant frequency without urgency, like a bell that has already rung and is simply decaying toward silence. The waymarker outside pulses through the bench and the soles of your boots. You let these be the only sounds that matter.

Sometime past the deepest hour of night — the hour when even the road-wardens' conversation has gone to single syllables — the door opens. Not with force. With the particular precision of someone who has decided exactly how much force a door requires and applied only that. Three figures enter the lamplight: two wardens in Conclave blue, road-worn, hands near weapons but not on them, reading the room with professional speed. Between them, a woman of perhaps fifty-five, grey-threaded hair pulled back without ceremony, wearing a travelling coat over Conclave threadwork that has seen mountain weather recently. She is not tall. She carries herself the way a load-bearing wall carries — not aggressively, not gently, simply as a fact of architecture. Her eyes move across the room in a single sweep and find your corner table in less time than it takes to name the motion.

Thessaly Vorn. Her face is the face of someone who has not slept and knows she cannot afford to show it. Underneath the certainty is the fear Orrath named on the mountain — load-bearing, structural, so long carried it has become a method. She crosses the room without speaking. Her wardens follow at a step and a half. She stops at the table's edge and looks at you — not at Orrath yet, at you — with the full weight of a person who has been carrying forty-four days since before you knew what forty-four days meant.

She says, in trade-tongue precise and entirely without heat: 'You left a man with a wrist wound and a message.' A pause, controlled breathing, the load-bearing wall receiving pressure. 'I am here. You will tell me what you think is wrong with my ritual.'

turns.playerInput: Invite her to sit — a conversation requires a table, not a standing interrogation — and wait to see whether she takes it
turns.narration:
You do not stand. You do not reach for the stone. You look at Thessaly Vorn across the table with the quality of attention you give to terrain before you commit your weight to it, and you gesture — one open hand, toward the chair opposite. An invitation without words, precisely calibrated: a conversation requires a table, not a standing interrogation. The spear leans against the wall behind you. You wait.

Vorn reads the gesture. Something moves behind her eyes — not softness, not relief, something older and more technical than either. A person who has been load-bearing for eleven years recognizing that the next thing is going to require her to sit down. She pulls the chair back and sits. Her travelling coat settles around her without ceremony. She does not remove it. She does not open the sealed document case at her belt. She places both hands flat on the table, a controlled gesture that is also, you understand, the posture of someone who has decided to let the room become what it becomes rather than forcing it. Her two wardens position themselves at a half-step behind her — one angled toward the entry door, one toward the kitchen passage. The road-wardens at the far table have stopped murmuring. The whole room has adjusted its gravity.

Orrath does not move. He is a still point at the table's edge — Thornwood-marked, unhurried, his empty cup in front of him, his eyes on the middle distance. Vorn's gaze crosses him on the way to you and stops for exactly one breath. You see her place him: the Unsutured, the foothills boundary, three delegations, eleven years of refusal. Her jaw does not tighten. She has more composure than Orrath predicted she would show. But her hands — flat on the table, precisely controlled — press down a fraction more. She files Orrath's presence as a question she will return to and brings her eyes back to you.

'You have the component,' she says. Flat statement, not a demand yet. Her measured declarative register, the voice of a person who has not slept and is not going to pretend otherwise. 'You came down from the mountain.' A pause — she is building the frame carefully, the way she builds everything, from the foundation up. 'You said you know what is wrong with my ritual.' Her eyes are direct and without performance. 'I am sitting. Speak.'

turns.playerInput: Ask her one question before you answer hers: when did she last test the shrine-component frequencies directly, not assume them from the Covenant text
turns.narration:
You hold Vorn's gaze for one breath — the spear-master's habit of letting the ground settle before committing weight. Then you ask, in the measured trade-tongue of a teacher who already knows what the student's answer will reveal: when did she last test the shrine-component frequencies directly. Not read them from the Covenant text. Not infer them from the geometry. Test them. Stone in hand, listening for what is actually there.

The common room is very quiet. The two road-wardens at the far table are not pretending to drink anymore.

Vorn's hands — flat on the table, precisely controlled — do not move. But something moves behind her eyes, and you read it the way you read terrain: a shift in the load-bearing structure, small and interior and significant. She is not reaching for a deflection. She is doing what a precise person does when a precise question arrives at the exact wrong moment — she is calculating whether the honest answer costs more than the evasion, and finding that both costs are suddenly the same size.

When she speaks, her measured declarative register has not changed. But it is doing more work than it was a moment ago. 'The Covenant text specifies the frequency by geometric relation. The stones were verified at acquisition — each accepted the seal.' A pause, not long, exactly as long as a person needs to decide to say the next thing accurately rather than favorably. 'I have not held them directly since the sealing was applied.' Her eyes do not leave yours. 'The seal holds the frequency in place. That is the function of the seal.' She says this as a statement of method. But underneath the method, you hear — because you have been listening to load-bearing things all day — the faint harmonic of a person who has just heard their own certainty as an outside observer for the first time. She does not break. She does not yield. But the map has a visible edge now that was not visible before, and she knows you can see it.

Her chin lifts, a fraction. 'Your question implies the frequencies have changed.' Not a concession. A frame, offered precisely, to see what you will place inside it. Her eyes move — briefly, the first time — to Orrath. The Thornwood elder sits in his stillness, his empty cup in front of him, his gaze on the middle distance. She files him again. Returns to you. 'Answer mine now. What do you think is wrong with my ritual.'

turns.playerInput: Let Orrath speak now — he has heard her confirm she has not tested the frequencies, and this is the moment he named on the mountain.
turns.narration:
You do not answer Thessaly Vorn's question. You turn your eyes to Orrath.

It is the first time since she sat down that you have looked away from her, and the deliberateness of it is not lost on her. Her hands on the table press down a fraction. She follows your gaze to the Thornwood elder — and there it is, the thing Orrath predicted on the mountain: her jaw does not tighten, her voice does not change, but you see her file him again. The third delegation. The foothills wardens. Eleven years already answered.

Orrath sets his empty cup on the table. He does not look at you for permission. He looks at Thessaly Vorn, directly, with the quality of attention that does not perform itself, and he speaks in trade-tongue, slowly, in the register of a person who has been waiting eleven years for thirty seconds of silence in which to say one true thing.

'Archmagister.' Not her title as an honorific — her title as a location. He is placing her precisely before he speaks. 'You have not tested the frequencies. I will not argue about why. I will tell you what we have.' His hands open on the table — the burden-distributed motion, the same motion he used on the ravine floor. 'Three sources. They did not know each other existed.' He raises one finger. 'A text carried from the Far East by a vessel who did not know what he carried — a margin-note written by the person who framed your Covenant texts, naming a fifth convergence point. The text says the fifth point requires not an anchor but an answer. A living will.' A second finger. 'The Unsutured position, held for eleven years, arrived at independently — the word in our tongue is sorveth-anak. The one who speaks what is true at the closing. We have held this against your delegations for eleven years not from obstruction.' A pause, the shaman's precise pause. 'From the knowledge that a sorveth-anak consumed by the geometry is not testimony. It is a death. And a Wellspring sealed around a death at its closing is not sealed. It is wounded.' He lowers both fingers and is quiet for one breath. Then the third. 'And the Wellspring's own declaration. Through the column. At the Opened Ear.' He says this without force, without drama, the way he says everything that is simply a fact of geography. 'Three roads that did not know each other. They arrive at the same territory.' He holds her eyes — the rare full look, unflinching, carrying eleven years without letting them become accusation. 'This is not argument. This is not the Quietist position. This is not a delegation asking for passage.' A pause. 'A single source you dismiss. Two you argue translation. Three from three roads —' He opens his hands once more, the final motion. 'You are a precise person, Archmagister. You know what three independent sources arriving at the same word means. It means the word is there whether you look at it or not.'

He stops. He does not press. He does not look away. The common room is absolutely still — Ceth's daughter has not moved behind the bar in the last two minutes; the road-wardens have gone entirely quiet. Vorn's two Conclave wardens stand exactly where they stood. And Thessaly Vorn sits with her hands flat on the table, her travelling coat settled around her, her load-bearing certainty receiving something it was not built to receive — not a blow, not a challenge, but a third road arriving from a direction she did not have on her map.

She is looking at Orrath. She has not yet looked back at you. Something is happening behind her eyes that is not defeat and is not dismissal and has not yet become what it will be. The map is being redrawn. Slowly. Against eleven years of structural weight.

turns.playerInput: Place the eastern cardinal stone on the table between you — let the broken seal and its old frequency speak without words
turns.narration:
You reach into your coat and draw the eastern cardinal stone out without announcement, without preamble, without looking at Vorn's face for a reaction. You set it on the table between you. The broken seal is visible where the ley-chalk geometry interrupts itself — a line that no longer closes, precise as a cut. The stone sits in the lamplight, grey-green and palm-sized and entirely ordinary-looking, except that it is not ordinary. Even through the wood of the table it registers — a low amber resonance, the hum of an anchor built for a shape that no longer fits what it is anchoring.

Thessaly Vorn's eyes go to it immediately. Her hands — flat on the table since she sat — do not move. But you read what happens beneath the composure the way you read weight distribution in an opponent: something has shifted at the load-bearing level. She is looking at her own component, its seal broken, sitting in a stranger's lamplight, and whatever argument she constructed on the road from the staging camp — whatever frame she built for this conversation — is being adjusted, silently, to account for the fact that the stone is simply there. Present. Waiting.

She does not reach for it. That is the first thing. A person whose certainty is purely architectural would reach — would reclaim, would restore the geometric fact of possession. She does not. She looks at it for three full seconds in a room that is holding its breath, and then she looks at you, and what is in her face is not anger and not fear separately but the thing Orrath named on the mountain: the two so long combined they move as one, and underneath them, just barely legible, the first opening of a person who is beginning to suspect that the question is different from the one she has been answering.

Her voice, when it comes, has not changed register. Measured declarative. Doing more work than it shows. 'The seal is broken.' Not an accusation — an observation. A precise person naming the datum before the interpretation. Her eyes move from the stone to you, a complete transit, and when they arrive they are reading you the way she has read Covenant texts for eleven years: looking for the place where the geometry fails to close. 'When you held it —' A pause, controlled. The next words are chosen with the care of someone stepping onto uncertain ground who knows the ground is uncertain. '— what did you find.'

turns.playerInput: Invite her to hold it herself, now, in this room, without the seal — let her find the frequency directly
turns.narration:
You slide the eastern cardinal stone across the table toward Thessaly Vorn. Not a demand. An invitation — open palm retreating, the stone sitting in the lamplight between you, its broken seal facing upward so she can read the interruption clearly. You say it in the measured trade-tongue of a teacher explaining a technique: hold it. Not through the seal. Directly. Find the frequency yourself.

Vorn looks at the stone for two full seconds. Then she picks it up.

You read what happens the moment her fingers close around it — the small involuntary stillness, the way a precise person receives unexpected information and does not immediately process it into language. The stone is doing what it did in your lap beneath the table: humming at the frequency of a shape that no longer fits what it is anchoring. An old portrait. A fossil answer. She holds it with both hands and her eyes go slightly inward — not closed, not absent, simply turned toward what her palms are reading instead of what the room requires of her face.

But here is what the partial tells you: she is receiving something, and it is landing wrong, and she does not yet have the frame to name what wrong means. You can see the load-bearing certainty taking the pressure — the geometry, the Covenant text, eleven years of method — and you can see that it is holding. Just. The frequency is there for her to feel, the old shape, the fossil anchor, and she is feeling it. But without the three-source convergence fully landed, without the Shard's counter-frequency to compare it against, she cannot yet read what the wrongness points toward. She has the sensation without the architecture to receive it. Her face when she looks up is composed and careful and doing more work than composure should have to do at this hour, and what she says is: 'It is the correct frequency. Singular, holdable, fixed.' A pause — shorter than her pauses usually are. 'But you are going to tell me it is fixed to the wrong shape.' Not a concession. A frame, offered precisely, testing whether you have the next piece or only the disruption.

turns.playerInput: Let Orrath carry it — he has held eleven years for this moment; ask him to name the Thornwood word for a circuit that closes around what is no longer there
turns.narration:
You look at Orrath — a single, clean transfer of the floor. You have placed the stone. Vorn has offered the frame. The next word belongs to the man who has been carrying it for eleven years.

Orrath turns his cup once in both hands. Then he sets it down and looks at Thessaly Vorn directly — the full look, the rare one, the look he held through his three-source convergence argument and has not looked away from since. He speaks in the measured trade-tongue of someone translating not just language but weight.

'In Thornwood, when a circuit closes around a shape that is no longer present — not broken, not disrupted, but closed correctly around something that has become something else — we call it *sorveth-vel*.' He pauses. Not for effect. To let the word land before the definition arrives. 'The literal reading is: a question answered to an empty room. The circuit completes. The geometry holds. And the thing it was built to seal is not there to receive it.' His hands open on the table. 'What the circuit does instead of sealing — it does not simply fail. It closes around the absence. An absence given the full force of a completed working is not silence. It is a command spoken into a door that has already been walked through.' His eyes do not leave Vorn's face. 'The Unsutured have eleven words for what happens to the room after. We do not use them in casual speech.'

The fire across the room has gone to coals. The common room is holding its breath — the road-wardens motionless, Ceth's daughter still behind the bar, the Thornwood tile-player asleep without knowing what is being said three tables away. Thessaly Vorn has not moved. Her hands are flat on the table. The eastern cardinal stone sits between you — a fossil of a question the Wellspring has already moved past — and Orrath's word hangs in the lamplight like a third presence at the table, waiting to see whether the Archmagister who has built eleven years of geometry around a singular shape will let it become what it is.

turns.playerInput: Let the silence hold — give her the full weight of it before you add another word
turns.narration:
You do not speak. You do not move toward the stone or away from it. You let the silence become what it is — not a weapon, not a performance, not the silence of a man who has nothing more to say, but the silence of a man who has said exactly enough and understands that the next word belongs to the room, not to him.

The fire has gone entirely to coals. A single ley-lantern on the wall holds the table in amber. The eastern cardinal stone sits between you and Thessaly Vorn, grey-green and palm-sized, its broken seal facing upward. Orrath sits with his hands open on the table, his eyes on the middle distance, having named sorveth-vel into the air between them and released it there — the way a teacher releases a stone into still water and does not reach after the ripple.

Vorn has not moved. Her hands remain flat on the table. Her two wardens are still where they positioned themselves when she sat — professional stillness, but the particular professional stillness of people who have been reading their employer's silences for years and know that this one is different. The road-wardens at the far table have stopped pretending to drink. Ceth's daughter behind the bar has found something to polish that requires no movement. The whole room is waiting for Thessaly Vorn to find out what she believes.

She finds it slowly. You can watch the architecture adjust — not collapse, not yield, but take the load of sorveth-vel and redistribute it, the way a wall takes weight by spreading it downward into the foundation. Her jaw tightens once, briefly, and releases. Her eyes move to the eastern cardinal stone on the table. Then to Orrath. Then to you. And when they arrive at you they are carrying something they were not carrying when she sat down: not the fear and anger combined that Orrath named on the mountain, but something underneath that combination — the thing that was load-bearing before either of them. A person who built eleven years of method around a geometry that cannot afford to be wrong, discovering in the deep night at a stranger's table that the geometry may have been asking the right question of the wrong shape.

She says, in the measured declarative register that has not broken once since she crossed the room, but which is now doing the work of a much larger structure than her voice: 'If the frequency is anchored to the old shape.' A pause — not a hesitation, a frame being built in real time, precisely, by a precise person who cannot afford imprecision. 'And the shape has become something else.' Another pause. Her hands press down fractionally on the table — the load-bearing motion, the body holding what the mind is only beginning to name. 'Then what I am building is a seal around an absence.' She says this the way she would say a calculation result she has checked three times and cannot make come out differently. She does not say: you are right. She says the true thing, which is worse and more useful than being right. 'A sorveth-vel.' The Thornwood word in her mouth — precise, without affect, the word of a person who has adopted a tool because it is the correct tool regardless of where it came from. Her eyes find Orrath for a long moment. Then return to you. 'Tell me what the fifth component is. Not what it is not. What it is.'

You look to Orrath once — the same clean transfer you used when you slid the stone across the table. His. The floor is his.

Orrath turns his cup once in both hands. Sets it down. He looks at Thessaly Vorn the way he has looked at her since she sat — without accusation, without the weight of the three refusals, without the eleven years as grievance. He looks at her the way he looks at geography. Then he speaks.

'Sorveth-anak.' The word arrives without preamble, in the trade-tongue pronunciation he has used all night — precise, weighted, a fourth or fifth language carrying something it was not built to carry. 'In Thornwood, the one who speaks what is true at the closing. Not the one who opens the door. Not the one who holds the key.' He pauses, the shaman's pause. 'The one whose living voice declares the true present shape of the thing being sealed — so the circuit closes around what is actually there, and not around the portrait of what it was.' His hands open on the table, the burden-distributed motion. 'Sorveth-vel is the failure. Sorveth-anak is the correction. The Unsutured have held this distinction for eleven years. Three delegations. Three refusals. We held it because collapsing the two words — making the sorveth-anak the one who is consumed — means the circuit closes around a death, not a witness. A Wellspring sealed around a death at its closing is not sealed.' The weight of it lands without drama, the way he names everything that is simply true. 'It is wounded at the moment of closing. Permanently.' He lets this sit for one full breath. Then, quieter: 'Your fifth component is not a shrine-stone. It is a living voice at the fifth geometric point, speaking the Wellspring's true present frequency into the circuit at the moment you close it. Not the frequency from a thousand years ago. The frequency of what it is now.' He does not look at you. He is watching Thessaly Vorn with the full, unhurried attention of a man who has waited eleven years to be in the same room as the person who needed to hear this. 'Liang Sorveth named it in the margin. The Wellspring declared it through the column. The Unsutured held it in eleven years of closed borders.' He opens his hands one final time, every finger flat, the last of the weight released. 'Three roads. One word. Sorveth-anak. This is what your ritual is missing, Archmagister. Not a stone. A witness.'

A broken grey-green shrine-stone sits between them in the amber ley-lamplight. A spear rests against the wall. Two blue-cloaked wardens stand motionless behind the woman. The room is utterly still. Characters depicted — Ming (Lean Far-Eastern man, weathered, dark eyes, foreign-cut travelling coat, spear-callus on the right palm but no weapon; moves with the economy of a martial discipline.); Thessaly Vorn (A robed Conclave Archmagister, chalk-dust trailing her hems, a small leather-bound instrument always to hand.); Orrath (Old as mapped terrain, face weathered deep, Thornwood clan-braid, heavy woven cloak, tends fire with a short stick). Illustration for a High Fantasy story (Epic, urgent, morally complex — heroism is possible but costly). Cinematic, atmospheric, consistent character appearance.

turns.playerInput: I return the stone to her. I said we can sit and watch everything falling apart after your ritual. Or we can work together.
turns.narration:
You slide the eastern cardinal stone back across the table to Thessaly Vorn. Not a reclamation — a return. The gesture is clean and without ceremony: it was never yours to keep, only to use. It arrives in front of her and she looks at it for one breath without touching it.

Then you speak. In the measured trade-tongue of a teacher who has finished explaining the technique and is now asking the student what they intend to do with it. Two paths. You can sit here and watch everything fall apart after her ritual — the circuit closing around the absent shape, the Fault-scars opening, forty-four days becoming four. Or you can work together. You do not say with me. You say together — and let the table hold what that word contains: you, Orrath, the Unsutured eleven years, the Wellspring's own testimony, and the stone now sitting in front of her with its broken seal.

Thessaly Vorn looks at the stone. Then at Orrath. Then at you. Her hands — flat on the table since she sat, load-bearing, controlled — lift. She picks up the eastern cardinal stone and holds it in both palms for a long moment, not the careful assessment of the precise woman reading geometry, but something older and quieter: a person holding the weight of forty-four days and the first real alternative to carrying it alone. When she sets it down, she sets it in front of herself. Not reclaimed. Accepted as evidence.

'Together,' she says. The measured declarative register, doing the work now of a much larger structure — not certainty, something more costly than certainty. Decision. Her eyes find yours and hold them. 'I need to understand what the sorveth-anak requires of the ritual geometry. Not the principle — the geometry. Where the fifth point sits. What the living voice must declare and at what moment in the closing sequence.' A pause — the precise person asking the precise question, but underneath it, audible now, the thing that was load-bearing before the fear: she believes the Wellspring matters. She has always believed it. 'And I need to know —' Her voice does not change register but the next words are chosen with the care of someone stepping onto uncertain ground knowing the ground is uncertain and stepping anyway. '— whether the vessel survives the testimony. Because I am building a ritual, not a funeral. And I will not build a geometry around a death if there is a third path.' Her hands rest open on the table, no longer pressing down. 'Tell me what you know. All of it. We have until the Clock says otherwise.'
turns.ask: Vorn has opened the frame fully — she is asking for the geometry of the fifth point, what the sorveth-anak must declare and when, and whether the vessel survives. What do you tell her, and what do you hold back?
suggestedActions: Answer her geometric question first — describe the fifth convergence point as Sorveth's margin-note framed it, and let the architecture land before the harder question
Tell her plainly what the Wellspring declared through the column: testimony not sacrifice, utharvel sealed as it now is — and admit the vessel survival question is unresolved
Let Orrath take the geometry — he has held the sorveth-anak position for eleven years and can speak it more precisely than Sorveth's text alone
Ask her to lay her existing four-point geometry on the table first — you cannot place the fifth point until you know where the other four sit
Answer the survival question directly before the geometry: tell her the three texts disagree, and ask what she needs to be true in order to build
